project: Vivekachudamani & Yoga Darshan — Instagram Reels Pilot Batch

reels.title: You Are Not Your Thoughts
reels.hook: What if I told you there are five layers hiding the real you... and your thoughts are only layer three?

script.visual: Close-up face, eyes wide, finger pointing at camera. Quick zoom-in.
script.voiceover: You are NOT your thoughts. And I can prove it.
script.text_overlay: YOU ARE NOT YOUR THOUGHTS

script.visual: Person peeling an onion, layer by layer. Or animation of concentric circles peeling away. Each layer labeled on screen.
script.voiceover: 2,500 years ago, a text called the Vivekachudamani said you have five layers — like sheaths around a sword. Layer one: your physical body — the Annamaya Kosha. Layer two: your breath and energy — Pranamaya.
script.text_overlay: Layer 1: Body (Annamaya) / Layer 2: Energy (Pranamaya)

script.visual: Person sitting, head in hands, thoughts visualized as floating text swirling around their head. Then a hand swatting them away.
script.voiceover: Layer three: your mind, your thoughts, your emotions — the Manomaya Kosha. This is where most people get stuck. They think THIS is who they are. But keep peeling. Layer four: your intellect, your ability to decide — Vijnanamaya. And layer five... pure bliss. Anandamaya.
script.text_overlay: Layer 3: Mind (Manomaya) / Layer 4: Intellect (Vijnanamaya) / Layer 5: Bliss (Anandamaya)

script.visual: Calm, centered shot of person looking directly into camera with soft light. Slow push-in.
script.voiceover: But here's the part that will break your brain: you're not even layer five. You are the awareness BEHIND all five layers. Shankara says the Atman — the real you — is the one watching all of it. You're not the onion. You're the one peeling it.
script.text_overlay: You are the AWARENESS behind all 5 layers

script.visual: Person closes eyes, slight smile. Camera slowly pulls back to wide shot.
script.voiceover: So next time your thoughts spiral — anxiety, doubt, overthinking — just remember: that's layer three talking. And you? You're way deeper than that.
script.text_overlay: That's just Layer 3.

script.visual: Text card on black background with follow prompt.
script.voiceover: Which layer do you get stuck on the most? Drop it in the comments.
script.text_overlay: Which layer traps you? Comment below

reels.cta: Which layer do you get stuck on the most? Comment below. Follow for more ancient wisdom that actually makes sense.
reels.caption:
You are not your thoughts. You are not your body. You're not even your bliss. You are the awareness BEHIND all of it.

The Vivekachudamani teaches the Pancha Kosha — five sheaths that hide your true Self. Most people live their entire lives stuck at layer 3 (the mind) thinking that's all there is.

Peel deeper.

#vivekachudamani #vedanta #advaita #panchakosha #selfawareness #spiritualawakening #ancientwisdom #hinduphilosophy #mindfulness #yogaphilosophy #atman #consciousness #innergrowth #knowthyself #spirituality
reels.music_suggestion: Soft ambient drone with a subtle bass drop at the 'brain break' moment (25s). Think Hans Zimmer meets lo-fi.

reels.title: The Imposter Syndrome Verse
reels.hook: A 2,500-year-old text just described your exact quarter-life crisis.

script.visual: Person staring at phone looking overwhelmed. Camera snaps to close-up.
script.voiceover: This was written over 2,000 years ago... and it sounds exactly like your last therapy session.
script.text_overlay: 2,000 YEARS AGO someone said THIS...

script.visual: Sanskrit text scrolling in background (blurred). Person reads 'translation' from phone as if discovering it.
script.voiceover: In the Vivekachudamani, a student goes to a teacher and says — and I'm paraphrasing here — 'I am drowning in the ocean of this worldly existence. I am burned by the fire of suffering. I am terrified. I don't know what to do. Please... save me.'
script.text_overlay: "I am drowning... burned by suffering... I don't know what to do."

script.visual: Split screen: ancient manuscript aesthetic on one side, modern person sitting on bed at 2am on the other.
script.voiceover: Sound familiar? That feeling of being lost? Of doing everything 'right' — school, job, relationships — and still feeling like something is deeply, fundamentally off? That's not a modern invention. That student felt it thousands of years ago. Shankara called it samsara — the cycle of confusion.
script.text_overlay: Samsara = the cycle of confusion you can't name

script.visual: Warm, hopeful lighting. Person looks up from their phone, slight realization on face.
script.voiceover: But here's what's beautiful: the teacher doesn't judge him. He says — 'The fact that you're asking this question? That you feel this discomfort? That IS the beginning of freedom.' Your confusion isn't weakness. It's the first sign of waking up.
script.text_overlay: Your confusion is the FIRST SIGN of waking up

script.visual: Person puts phone down. Looks at camera, calm. End card.
script.voiceover: So if you're feeling lost right now — good. That means you're ready. Save this for when you need it.
script.text_overlay: Save this for when you need it

reels.cta: Save this for a rough day. Tag someone who needs to hear this right now.
reels.caption:
2,500 years ago, a student walked up to a teacher and basically said: 'I have no idea what I'm doing with my life and everything hurts.'

The teacher's response? 'Good. That means you're ready.'

Vivekachudamani, verses 35-40. The original imposter syndrome confessional.

Your confusion is not your enemy. It's your invitation.

#vivekachudamani #vedanta #impostersyndrome #quarterlifecrisis #spiritualawakening #lostinlife #advaita #shankara #selfdiscovery #ancientwisdom #mentalhealth #findingpurpose #innerpeace #philosophy #healing
reels.music_suggestion: Melancholic piano intro that shifts to hopeful at the 28-second mark. Soft strings entering at the resolution.

reels.title: What is Maya?
reels.hook: You're living in a simulation. No, seriously — ancient India figured this out before Elon Musk.

script.visual: Glitch effect on screen. Person appears to 'glitch' in and out. Matrix-style green text for a flash.
script.voiceover: You're living in a simulation. And no, I'm not talking about Elon Musk.
script.text_overlay: You're living in a SIMULATION

script.visual: Person holding a rope. Camera starts wide then slowly zooms to reveal it's just a rope — but at the start, the lighting makes it look like a snake.
script.voiceover: The Vivekachudamani describes something called Maya — and the classic example is this: you walk into a dark room. You see a snake on the floor. Your heart races. You panic. Then someone turns on the light. It was a rope the whole time. The snake was never real.
script.text_overlay: MAYA = seeing a snake where there's only a rope

script.visual: Quick montage: checking social media likes, comparing ourselves to others, chasing promotions, buying things. Fast cuts, slightly frantic energy.
script.voiceover: Maya is the force that makes you see what isn't there. It makes you think happiness is in that promotion, that relationship, that purchase. It makes you think YOU are your job title, your bank account, your follower count. It's not that these things don't exist — it's that they're not what they appear to be.
script.text_overlay: Maya doesn't mean 'nothing is real' / It means 'nothing is what it APPEARS to be'

script.visual: Calm shot. Person in natural light, speaking directly and gently to camera.
script.voiceover: Shankara says Maya has two powers. One: it hides reality from you — called Avarana, the veiling power. Two: it projects a false reality in its place — called Vikshepa. So first it blindfolds you, then it shows you a movie and tells you THAT'S real.
script.text_overlay: Avarana = hides the truth / Vikshepa = projects the illusion

script.visual: Person slowly removes sunglasses, looks at camera with clarity.
script.voiceover: The way out? Viveka — the ability to tell the real from the unreal. That's literally the title of this whole text. The Crest Jewel of Discrimination.
script.text_overlay: VIVEKA = telling real from unreal

script.visual: End card, text prompt.
script.voiceover: What's one thing you used to chase that turned out to be a rope? Tell me below.
script.text_overlay: What was YOUR rope? Comment below

reels.cta: What did you chase that turned out to be a rope? Share in the comments. Follow for more decoded philosophy.
reels.caption:
Maya doesn't mean 'the world is fake.'

It means: you're seeing a snake where there's only a rope.

The Vivekachudamani says Maya has two superpowers:
1. Avarana — it HIDES reality from you
2. Vikshepa — it PROJECTS a false reality in its place

First it blindfolds you. Then it plays you a movie and says 'this is real.'

The way out? Viveka. Learning to tell the rope from the snake.

#maya #vivekachudamani #vedanta #advaita #illusion #shankara #spiritualawakening #philosophy #ancientwisdom #hinduphilosophy #consciousness #realvsunreal #matrixtheory #selfrealization #wakeup
reels.music_suggestion: Eerie, suspenseful synth for the first 15 seconds (snake reveal), transitioning to warm acoustic after the 'turn on the light' moment.

reels.title: The 4 Things You Need Before You Can Find Yourself
reels.hook: There are 4 prerequisites to knowing who you really are. Most people have zero.

script.visual: Person holding up 4 fingers, dead serious face. Quick zoom.
script.voiceover: There are exactly four things you need before you can find yourself. And most people have zero.
script.text_overlay: 4 PREREQUISITES to knowing who you are

script.visual: Numbered list appearing on screen, one at a time. Person pacing and explaining energetically.
script.voiceover: The Vivekachudamani lays out four requirements — called Sadhana Chatushtaya. Number one: Viveka — discrimination. The ability to tell what's permanent from what's temporary. Your job? Temporary. Your body? Temporary. That ego you protect so hard? Temporary. Can you see what remains when all of that is gone?
script.text_overlay: 1. VIVEKA = Real vs. Temporary

script.visual: Person physically pushing things away — phone, wallet, mirror. Symbolic and slightly humorous.
script.voiceover: Number two: Vairagya — dispassion. Not renouncing the world, but not being owned by it. Can you enjoy things without needing them? There's a huge difference between 'I like this' and 'I can't survive without this.'
script.text_overlay: 2. VAIRAGYA = Enjoy without clinging

script.visual: Person sitting down, ticking off a mental checklist. Calm, focused energy.
script.voiceover: Number three: Shat-Sampatti — the six virtues. Mental calm, self-control, withdrawal from distractions, endurance, faith, and focus. Think of it as emotional fitness training. And number four: Mumukshutva — an intense, burning desire for liberation. Not a casual 'yeah, enlightenment sounds cool.' I mean a fire-in-your-chest, I-refuse-to-stay-asleep kind of longing.
script.text_overlay: 3. SHAT-SAMPATTI = 6 mental virtues / 4. MUMUKSHUTVA = Burning desire for freedom

script.visual: Person looks directly into camera, sincere, slightly intense.
script.voiceover: Here's the thing — Shankara isn't gatekeeping. He's telling you these aren't talents. They're muscles. You build them. Every time you choose awareness over autopilot, you're training Viveka. Every time you let go of something that's controlling you, that's Vairagya.
script.text_overlay: These aren't talents. They're MUSCLES.

script.visual: End card.
script.voiceover: Which of these four do you need to work on most? Comment your number.
script.text_overlay: Your weakest? Drop 1, 2, 3, or 4

reels.cta: Drop 1, 2, 3, or 4 in the comments for the one you need to build most. Follow for the full breakdown of each one.
reels.caption:
Before you can know who you truly are, you need 4 things.

The Vivekachudamani calls them Sadhana Chatushtaya:

1. VIVEKA - Can you tell what's real from what's temporary?
2. VAIRAGYA - Can you enjoy life without being owned by it?
3. SHAT-SAMPATTI - Do you have mental discipline?
4. MUMUKSHUTVA - Do you BURN for freedom?

These aren't gifts. They're muscles. Build them.

Which one is your weakest? Drop your number.

#sadhanachatushtaya #vivekachudamani #vedanta #advaita #spiritualgrowth #selfdiscovery #shankara #ancientwisdom #vairagya #viveka #mumukshutva #discipline #innerwork #philosophy #consciousness
reels.music_suggestion: Upbeat, motivational lo-fi with a driving beat. Think 'get things done' energy — not meditation music.

reels.title: Tat Tvam Asi — You Are That
reels.hook: Three words that could end your identity crisis forever.

script.visual: Black screen. Three Sanskrit words appear one at a time with a bass hit on each: TAT. TVAM. ASI. Then cut to person's face.
script.voiceover: Three words. They could end your identity crisis forever.
script.text_overlay: TAT TVAM ASI

script.visual: Person speaking intimately, close-up. Warm lighting, almost like a late-night conversation.
script.voiceover: Tat Tvam Asi. It's Sanskrit. It means 'You Are That.' It comes from the Chandogya Upanishad and it's THE central teaching in the Vivekachudamani. But what does it actually mean? Let me explain it the way a teacher would explain it to a 25-year-old having an existential crisis.
script.text_overlay: "You Are That" — but what does that MEAN?

script.visual: Two side-by-side images: a single wave, and an ocean. Slowly merge them together.
script.voiceover: Imagine you're a wave in the ocean. You spend your whole life thinking 'I'm this wave. I'm separate. I was born, I'll crash, and I'll die.' But Tat Tvam Asi says: you were never just the wave. You ARE the ocean. The wave is a temporary shape. The water? That's what you've always been. Tat — that infinite consciousness. Tvam — you, right now, reading this. Asi — are. You. Are. That.
script.text_overlay: TAT = infinite consciousness / TVAM = you / ASI = are

script.visual: Person getting animated, leaning forward. Energy picks up.
script.voiceover: Shankara says the reason you don't see this is because of two things: you identify with the wave — your name, your body, your story — and you think 'That' is something far away, something unreachable, something for monks on mountains. But the whole point of Tat Tvam Asi is: there's no distance to travel. You're already there. You've always been there.
script.text_overlay: There's nowhere to go. You're already THAT.

script.visual: Slow motion of ocean waves. Person's voice softens.
script.voiceover: You don't become enlightened. You just stop pretending you're only a wave.
script.text_overlay: You don't become enlightened. You stop pretending you're only a wave.

script.visual: End card on black.
script.voiceover: Send this to someone who needs to remember what they really are.
script.text_overlay: Send this to someone who forgot who they are

reels.cta: Send this to someone who's been feeling small. Remind them what they really are. Follow for more.
reels.caption:
TAT TVAM ASI.

Three words from the Vivekachudamani that could change everything.

You are not the wave. You are the ocean.

Tat = That (infinite consciousness)
Tvam = You (yes, you, reading this right now)
Asi = Are

You. Are. That.

You don't become enlightened. You stop pretending you're only a wave.

Send this to someone who forgot.

#tattvamasi #vivekachudamani #vedanta #advaita #mahavakya #shankara #youarethat #consciousness #enlightenment #spiritualawakening #ancientwisdom #hinduphilosophy #selfrealization #atman #brahman
reels.music_suggestion: Deep, oceanic ambient with gentle waves layered in. Crescendo at 'You Are That' moments. Peaceful resolution at the end.

reels.title: Yoga is NOT What You Think
reels.hook: Yoga has nothing to do with your body. Here's what Patanjali actually said.

script.visual: Quick montage of Instagram yoga poses — handstands, splits, beach yoga. Then RECORD SCRATCH. Hard cut to person's face.
script.voiceover: Yoga has nothing to do with your body.
script.text_overlay: YOGA has NOTHING to do with your body

script.visual: Person holding up phone showing yoga influencer posts, then putting it face-down on table.
script.voiceover: I know, I know — every yoga account on Instagram is just people in expensive leggings doing backbends on a cliff. But when Patanjali wrote the Yoga Sutras — literally THE foundational text of yoga — the SECOND line says this: Yogash chitta vritti nirodhah.
script.text_overlay: Sutra 1.2: Yogash chitta vritti nirodhah

script.visual: Animation or visual of a lake: first choppy and turbulent, then perfectly still. Reflection becomes clear.
script.voiceover: Translation: Yoga is the cessation of the fluctuations of the mind. That's it. Not 'yoga is a really good hamstring stretch.' YOGA IS MASTERING YOUR MIND. Think of your mind like a lake. Right now, it's choppy — thoughts, worries, memories, fantasies, all splashing around. You can't see the bottom. Yoga is making the water still so you can finally see clearly.
script.text_overlay: Yoga = stilling the mind / Chitta = mind-stuff / Vritti = fluctuations / Nirodhah = cessation

script.visual: Person gesturing emphatically but warmly. Conversational energy.
script.voiceover: The physical postures — asana — that's just ONE of eight limbs. And Patanjali gives it exactly THREE sutras out of 196. Three! The rest is about your mind, your behavior, your focus, your surrender. We turned an entire science of consciousness into a fitness class.
script.text_overlay: Asana = 3 sutras out of 196 / That's 1.5%

script.visual: Person sitting simply, cross-legged, eyes closed briefly, then opens and looks at camera.
script.voiceover: Real yoga isn't about touching your toes. It's about what you learn on the way down. And it starts right here — between your ears. What does yoga mean to YOU? Tell me in the comments.
script.text_overlay: Real yoga starts between your ears. What's yoga to YOU?

reels.cta: What does yoga mean to you? Drop your honest answer below. Follow for the real Yoga Sutras, decoded.
reels.caption:
Patanjali didn't write 196 sutras about handstands.

Sutra 1.2: Yogash chitta vritti nirodhah
= Yoga is the cessation of the fluctuations of the mind

Asana (physical postures) gets exactly 3 sutras out of 196. That's 1.5%.

The other 98.5%? Your mind. Your ego. Your focus. Your freedom.

We turned an entire science of consciousness into a fitness class.

Time to go back to the source.

#yogasutras #patanjali #yogadarshan #yogaphilosophy #realyoga #yogaisnotasana #chittavrittinirodhah #mindfulness #meditation #consciousness #ancientwisdom #spiritualawakening #yogalife #ashtangayoga #yogateacher
reels.music_suggestion: Start with trendy upbeat music (matching yoga influencer montage), then abrupt switch to silence/single singing bowl at the 'record scratch' moment. Soft ambient for the rest.

reels.title: The 5 Mental Patterns That Control Your Life
reels.hook: Your mind only does 5 things. And once you see them, you can't unsee it.

script.visual: Person taps side of head. Five fingers up. Intense eye contact.
script.voiceover: Your mind only does five things. And once you see the pattern, you can't unsee it.
script.text_overlay: Your mind only does 5 THINGS

script.visual: Quick-fire text appearing on screen: 'Right Knowledge, Wrong Knowledge, Imagination, Sleep, Memory' — each word pops in with a beat.
script.voiceover: Patanjali says every single mental activity — every thought, every daydream, every worry — falls into one of five categories. He calls them the Pancha Vrittis. Let me break them down so fast you'll start categorizing your own thoughts in real time.
script.text_overlay: THE 5 VRITTIS (mental patterns)

script.visual: Split screen examples. Left: the vritti name. Right: relatable modern example.
script.voiceover: One — Pramana — right knowledge. When you see something accurately as it is. 'That's a chair. The sky is blue.' Factual, verified, true. Two — Viparyaya — wrong knowledge. Misperception. 'My boss hates me.' Maybe. Or maybe she just had a bad morning. You're SURE you're right... but you're building on a lie.
script.text_overlay: 1. PRAMANA = Right knowledge / 2. VIPARYAYA = Wrong knowledge (you THINK it's true)

script.visual: Person staring into space (imagination), then sleeping, then looking at an old photo (memory). Quick, relatable cuts.
script.voiceover: Three — Vikalpa — imagination. Stuff that only exists in words and concepts. 'What if I fail?' 'What if they leave?' It hasn't happened. It may never happen. But your body reacts as if it's real. Four — Nidra — deep sleep. Even that is a vritti — the experience of nothingness. Five — Smriti — memory. Pulling the past into the present. And sometimes refusing to let go.
script.text_overlay: 3. VIKALPA = Imagination / 4. NIDRA = Sleep / 5. SMRITI = Memory

script.visual: Person counting on fingers, looking slightly amazed. Then calm, centered shot.
script.voiceover: That's it. Five. Every thought you've ever had fits in one of these boxes. And here's the power move: Patanjali says each one can either cause you suffering or not. The vritti itself isn't the problem. Your ATTACHMENT to it is. When you can watch a thought arise, label it — 'oh, that's Vikalpa, that's just imagination' — and let it pass? That's yoga. That's freedom.
script.text_overlay: The vritti isn't the problem. Your ATTACHMENT to it is.

script.visual: End card.
script.voiceover: Right now, in this moment — which vritti is running? Drop your answer. Follow for more mind decoded.
script.text_overlay: Which vritti is running RIGHT NOW? 1-5?

reels.cta: Which vritti is running in your head right now? Drop 1-5. Follow to decode your own mind.
reels.caption:
Patanjali said your mind only has 5 modes:

1. PRAMANA - Right knowledge (seeing clearly)
2. VIPARYAYA - Wrong knowledge (you're sure... but wrong)
3. VIKALPA - Imagination (it hasn't happened yet)
4. NIDRA - Sleep (yes, this counts)
5. SMRITI - Memory (the past on replay)

Every single thought fits in one of these.

The thought isn't the problem. Your attachment to it is.

Label it. Watch it. Let it pass. That's yoga.

#yogasutras #patanjali #vrittis #mindpatterns #yogaphilosophy #consciousness #mentalhealth #selfawareness #meditation #ancientwisdom #yogadarshan #thoughtpatterns #mindfulness #innergrowth #psychology
reels.music_suggestion: Uptempo lo-fi with a 'think fast' energy for the list section (3-35s), slowing to calm ambient for the reflection portion.

reels.title: Why You Can't Stop Overthinking
reels.hook: Patanjali diagnosed your overthinking 2,000 years ago. He even listed the symptoms.

script.visual: Person lying in bed staring at ceiling. Alarm clock shows 3:17 AM. Hard cut to sitting up and looking at camera.
script.voiceover: Can't stop overthinking? Patanjali diagnosed you 2,000 years ago.
script.text_overlay: Patanjali DIAGNOSED your overthinking

script.visual: Person at desk, trying to focus. Thought bubbles (text overlays) keep popping up around their head: 'did I send that email?' 'what did they mean by that?' 'am I wasting my life?'
script.voiceover: In the Yoga Sutras, Patanjali lists nine obstacles to inner peace. He calls them Antarayas. And one of them is Vikshepa — mental distraction. Not just 'I got distracted by my phone.' Deep, structural inability to hold your mind on one thing. Your attention is scattered. Your mind jumps from thought to thought like a monkey on caffeine.
script.text_overlay: VIKSHEPA = scattered mind / (monkey on caffeine)

script.visual: Person grabs their head in recognition. Then calms down, leans forward conspiratorially.
script.voiceover: But here's what's wild — Patanjali doesn't just name the disease. He lists the SYMPTOMS. Sutra 1.31 says when Vikshepa hits, you get: mental pain, negativity, physical restlessness, and disturbed breathing. Sound familiar? That anxious feeling in your chest? The racing thoughts at 3 AM? The inability to sit still? That's not a character flaw. It's Vikshepa. It has a name.
script.text_overlay: Symptoms: pain, negativity, restlessness, disturbed breathing

script.visual: Person demonstrates the solution visually: takes one deep breath, focuses gaze on a single point (like a candle flame or dot on wall).
script.voiceover: And the cure? Sutra 1.32: Eka tattva abhyasa. Practice on a single principle. One thing. Pick one focus — your breath, a mantra, a single idea — and bring your mind back to it. Again. And again. And again. Not because you're bad at focusing. Because that's literally the practice. Every time you catch your mind wandering and bring it back? That rep IS the workout.
script.text_overlay: THE CURE: Eka tattva abhyasa = focus on ONE thing / Every 'bring it back' IS the practice

script.visual: Person sitting peacefully, slight smile. End card.
script.voiceover: Your overthinking has a 2,000-year-old name and a 2,000-year-old cure. Try it tonight: one focus, five minutes. Tell me how it goes.
script.text_overlay: Tonight: 1 focus. 5 minutes. Report back.

reels.cta: Try it tonight: pick one focus, sit for 5 minutes. Come back and tell me what happened. Follow for the ancient operating manual for your mind.
reels.caption:
Patanjali called it VIKSHEPA.

Your scattered, restless, overthinking mind isn't a character flaw. It's an obstacle he identified 2,000 years ago.

He even listed the symptoms (Sutra 1.31):
- Mental pain
- Negativity
- Physical restlessness
- Disturbed breathing

Sound like your 3 AM anxiety? Yeah.

The cure (Sutra 1.32): Eka tattva abhyasa. Focus on ONE thing. Bring your mind back. Again. And again.

Every 'bring it back' IS the practice.

Try it tonight. 5 minutes. Report back.

#yogasutras #patanjali #overthinking #vikshepa #anxietyrelief #meditation #mindfulness #yogaphilosophy #mentalhealth #focus #concentration #ancientwisdom #yogadarshan #innerpeace #consciousness
reels.music_suggestion: Anxious, glitchy electronic for the first 15 seconds, dissolving into a single sustained singing bowl note for the solution section.

reels.title: The 5 Root Causes of ALL Your Suffering
reels.hook: Every problem you've ever had traces back to one of these five roots. Every. Single. One.

script.visual: Person draws a tree on a whiteboard/paper. Writes 'SUFFERING' on the branches. Taps the roots.
script.voiceover: Every problem you've ever had? It has a root. And there are only five.
script.text_overlay: 5 ROOT CAUSES of all your suffering

script.visual: Camera tight on person's face. Serious but compassionate. Counting on fingers.
script.voiceover: Patanjali calls them the Kleshas — the five afflictions. And they're savage in how accurate they are. Number one: Avidya — ignorance. Not 'you're dumb' ignorance. Cosmic ignorance. Mistaking the temporary for the permanent. Thinking your body, your status, your story IS you. This is the root of all the other four.
script.text_overlay: 1. AVIDYA = Ignorance (the ROOT of all roots)

script.visual: Person looks in mirror, adjusts hair, puffs up chest — then deflates. Shows both pride and insecurity.
script.voiceover: Number two: Asmita — ego. The 'I-maker.' That voice that says 'I am this body, I am this personality, I am better than, I am worse than.' It creates a self-image and then spends all its energy defending it. Number three: Raga — attachment. Craving what feels good. Clinging to pleasure. 'I need this person, this outcome, this feeling to be okay.'
script.text_overlay: 2. ASMITA = Ego (the I-maker) / 3. RAGA = Attachment (craving pleasure)

script.visual: Person pushing things away, wincing. Then curled up protectively.
script.voiceover: Number four: Dvesha — aversion. The opposite of Raga. Pushing away what's painful. Avoiding hard conversations, hard truths, hard feelings. Running from discomfort instead of facing it. And number five — the deepest one — Abhinivesha — the fear of death. The clinging to life that's so deep, Patanjali says even wise people feel it. Every anxiety you have? Trace it back far enough and it's this: the fear that you will end.
script.text_overlay: 4. DVESHA = Aversion (running from pain) / 5. ABHINIVESHA = Fear of death (the deepest one)

script.visual: Person sits down. Calm, grounded. Draws the tree again but now labels the five roots.
script.voiceover: Here's what's liberating about this: once you can NAME the root, it loses power. Next time you're anxious, ask: is this Avidya — am I confused about what's real? Is this Raga — am I clinging? Is this Dvesha — am I running? Just naming it creates space between you and the suffering. That space is where freedom lives.
script.text_overlay: NAME IT = TAME IT / The space between you and the klesha is freedom

script.visual: End card.
script.voiceover: Which klesha runs your life the most? Be honest. Drop it below.
script.text_overlay: Which klesha runs YOUR life? 1-5

reels.cta: Which klesha controls you the most? Drop 1-5 in the comments. Honesty is the first step. Follow for the ancient psychology playbook.
reels.caption:
Patanjali said ALL suffering has 5 root causes. He called them the KLESHAS:

1. AVIDYA - Ignorance (confusing temporary for permanent)
2. ASMITA - Ego (the 'I-maker' that builds and defends your self-image)
3. RAGA - Attachment (clinging to what feels good)
4. DVESHA - Aversion (running from what feels bad)
5. ABHINIVESHA - Fear of death (the deepest one — even wise people feel it)

Every anxiety, every conflict, every pain traces back to one of these five.

Naming it creates space. Space is freedom.

Which one runs your life? Be honest.

#kleshas #yogasutras #patanjali #suffering #avidya #ego #attachment #yogaphilosophy #consciousness #mentalhealth #selfawareness #ancientwisdom #yogadarshan #psychology #innerwork
reels.music_suggestion: Dark, introspective piano for the first 37 seconds. Shifts to open, spacious ambient (maybe a single flute note) for the 'naming it creates space' section. Hopeful ending.

reels.title: The 8 Steps to Master Your Mind
reels.hook: There's an 8-step system to master your mind. Step 3 is the only one most people know.

script.visual: Eight stairs drawn/shown. Person standing at the bottom looking up. Camera tilts up.
script.voiceover: There's an 8-step system to master your mind. And step 3 is the only one most people know.
script.text_overlay: 8 STEPS to master your mind / (you only know Step 3)

script.visual: Person climbing literal steps or walking through 8 marked zones. Energetic, quick-paced.
script.voiceover: Patanjali's Ashtanga Yoga. No, not the fitness class — the original eight-limbed path. And it starts way before you ever get on a mat. Step one: Yama — how you treat others. Non-violence, truthfulness, non-stealing, moderation, non-possessiveness. Step two: Niyama — how you treat yourself. Cleanliness, contentment, discipline, self-study, surrender.
script.text_overlay: 1. YAMA = Ethics (how you treat others) / 2. NIYAMA = Discipline (how you treat yourself)

script.visual: Person strikes a yoga pose, then immediately sits back down. Playful energy.
script.voiceover: Step three: Asana — the physical postures. YES, that yoga. This is where 99% of the Western world stopped. But Patanjali describes it in literally one sutra: 'Steady, comfortable seat.' That's it. Sit comfortably. Cool. Moving on.
script.text_overlay: 3. ASANA = Steady comfortable seat / (that's literally all Patanjali said)

script.visual: Person demonstrates breathing, then shows withdrawing senses (putting in earplugs, closing eyes — humorous but illustrative).
script.voiceover: Step four: Pranayama — breath control. Master your breath, master your energy. Step five: Pratyahara — withdrawing your senses. Your eyes, ears, taste, touch — they're constantly pulling your attention outward. Pratyahara is the skill of turning inward. Think of it as the bridge between the outer practices and the inner ones.
script.text_overlay: 4. PRANAYAMA = Breath mastery / 5. PRATYAHARA = Withdrawing senses (the bridge)

script.visual: Person sits still. Camera slowly zooms in on their face as they describe the inner three. Visuals get more minimal, more focused.
script.voiceover: Now the deep stuff. Step six: Dharana — concentration. Holding your mind on one single point. Step seven: Dhyana — meditation. When that concentration becomes a continuous, effortless flow. And step eight: Samadhi — absorption. When you and the object of your focus merge. There is no 'you' meditating anymore. Just pure awareness. The mind isn't just quiet — it's transparent.
script.text_overlay: 6. DHARANA = Concentration / 7. DHYANA = Meditation / 8. SAMADHI = Absorption (pure awareness)

script.visual: Camera pulls back. Person is centered, calm.
script.voiceover: Eight steps. Ethics first. Postures in the middle. Transcendence at the end. The system was always complete. We just skipped to step three and called it done.
script.text_overlay: The system was always complete. We just stopped at Step 3.

script.visual: End card.
script.voiceover: Which step are you on? And which one are you skipping? Be real with me. Comment below.
script.text_overlay: Which step are you on? Which are you skipping?

reels.cta: Which step are you actually on? And which one are you conveniently skipping? Be real. Comment below. Follow for the full deep dive on each limb.
reels.caption:
Patanjali's ASHTANGA YOGA - the original 8-step system:

1. YAMA - How you treat others (ethics)
2. NIYAMA - How you treat yourself (discipline)
3. ASANA - Physical postures (this is where most people stop)
4. PRANAYAMA - Breath control
5. PRATYAHARA - Withdrawing the senses
6. DHARANA - Concentration
7. DHYANA - Meditation
8. SAMADHI - Absorption (pure awareness)

Notice: ethics comes FIRST. Postures come THIRD. Transcendence comes LAST.

The system was always complete. We just stopped at step 3 and called it yoga.

Which step are you on? Which are you skipping?

#ashtangayoga #yogasutras #patanjali #eightlimbs #yogaphilosophy #dharana #dhyana #samadhi #pranayama #pratyahara #yama #niyama #meditation #consciousness #ancientwisdom #yogadarshan #spiritualgrowth
reels.music_suggestion: Building energy — starts with a simple heartbeat-like pulse, adds layers with each step. Reaches full, expansive orchestral/ambient at Samadhi (step 8). Drops to silence for the closing question.
